sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Randy Gutierrez, a 2-year-old male, has been experiencing symptoms of a viral upper respiratory infection for approximately 1 day, starting around supper time last night.
He has a runny nose, is stuffy, and has a cough, but it is not a barking cough.
He has also been pulling on his ears and rubbing his nose.
His rectal temperature was 100.3°F.
He has had a decreased appetite, eating only 7 ounces of formula this morning and refusing cereal.
He has been irritable and having difficulty sleeping.
He has been exposed to his older sister, who has been sick, and a playdate with a nephew who had a sinus infection.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: Saline mist for nasal congestion, Tylenol (dosage not specified) given this morning for fever, Baby Vicks on his feet, and a humidifier.
Objective:
Vital signs: Temperature 100.3°F, no other vital signs mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's ears looked okay, but the cradle cap has worsened.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a viral upper respiratory infection.
Problem list: Upper respiratory infection; Cradle cap; Allergic to honey.
Plan:
I prescribed Zarbee's for the stuffy nose, and the same medicine as his sister for a barky cough, which is a half a teaspoon, to be given if the cough becomes barking.
I also recommended using Head & Shoulders, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral AD shampoo for the cradle cap, to be used two times a week, and to continue using a soft brush and baby oil to remove scales.
Follow-up:
We will see the patient in one week, and he will be discharged.
